name: Bean Common Mosaic Virus (BCMV)
symptoms: Mosaic pattern of light and dark green on leaves
Leaf curling and distortion
Stunted plant growth and reduced leaf size
Yellowing between leaf veins (interveinal chlorosis)
Pods may be few, small, and discolored
treatment: Remove and destroy infected plants immediately to prevent spread
Apply insecticides (imidacloprid or thiamethoxam) to control aphid vectors
Use reflective mulch to deter aphids from landing on plants
prevention: Plant certified virus-free and BCMV-resistant bean varieties (e.g. RWR 2245)
Control aphid populations with regular monitoring and targeted spraying
Avoid planting near infected fields or other legume crops
imageDescription: Leaves show irregular mosaic pattern with patches of light green and dark green. Leaves are smaller than normal, puckered, and curled downward. Plants are noticeably stunted compared to healthy plants.

name: Angular Leaf Spot (Pseudocercospora griseola)
symptoms: Water-soaked angular lesions on leaves bounded by leaf veins
Lesions turn brown to grey with a reddish-brown border
Infected leaves turn yellow and drop prematurely
Dark brown streaks on stems and pods
White-grey powdery fungal growth visible on lesions under humid conditions
treatment: Apply copper-based fungicides (copper oxychloride or copper hydroxide) at first sign of disease
Spray mancozeb or chlorothalonil every 7-10 days during wet conditions
Remove and destroy heavily infected plant debris to reduce inoculum
prevention: Use certified disease-free or resistant bean seed varieties (e.g. MAC 44, CODMLB 001)
Practice crop rotation — avoid growing beans in the same field for 2 consecutive seasons
Ensure adequate plant spacing (30cm x 50cm) for good air circulation
imageDescription: Lesions are angular or rectangular in shape, sharply delimited by leaf veins, turning from water-soaked to a grey-brown colour. In humid weather, a grey powdery sporulation is visible on the underside of leaves.

name: Bean Rust (Uromyces appendiculatus)
symptoms: Small, circular, reddish-brown pustules (uredinia) on undersides of leaves
Corresponding pale yellow spots on the upper leaf surface
Heavy infection causes yellowing and premature leaf drop
Pustules may also appear on stems and pods
White ring of tissue surrounds each pustule in early infection
treatment: Apply triazole fungicides (tebuconazole or propiconazole) at early disease onset
Use mancozeb or sulfur-based fungicide sprays every 10-14 days
Remove and burn severely infected crop material to reduce spore load
prevention: Use rust-resistant bean varieties registered in Rwanda (e.g. RWV 3006, Lyamungu 85)
Avoid overhead irrigation which creates leaf wetness that promotes spore germination
Monitor fields regularly from flowering stage onwards for early detection
imageDescription: Numerous small circular pustules appear on leaf undersides with a powdery, rust-red to brown spore mass. Upper leaf surfaces show corresponding chlorotic yellow spots. Heavy infections cause a rusted appearance and severe defoliation.

name: Anthracnose (Colletotrichum lindemuthianum)
symptoms: Dark brown to black lesions along the veins on the underside of leaves
Reddish-brown sunken cankers on stems and pods
Infected pods develop dark, circular sunken spots with salmon-pink spore masses
Seeds become discolored or shrivel within infected pods
Seedling death (damping-off) in severe early infection
treatment: Apply carbendazim or thiophanate-methyl fungicide at 7-day intervals during wet weather
Use copper-based fungicides as a protectant spray before and during rainy periods
Remove and destroy all infected plant material including crop debris after harvest
prevention: Always use certified anthracnose-free seed from reputable agro-dealers
Treat seeds with thiram or captan seed dressing before planting
Avoid working in the field when plants are wet to prevent mechanical spread
imageDescription: Pods show dark, sunken, circular lesions with a distinctive pink to salmon-coloured gelatinous spore mass in humid conditions. Veins on the underside of leaves turn dark brown or black. Stems develop elongated brown cankers.

name: Bean Stem Maggot (Ophiomyia spp.)
symptoms: Minute yellow egg-laying punctures visible on young leaves
Leaf mining tunnels visible as pale serpentine lines in leaves
Stem tunnelling causing wilting, yellowing, and plant death
Swollen stem base with larvae visible when split open
Sudden collapse of apparently healthy young plants (stem girdling stage)
treatment: Apply systemic insecticides such as dimethoate or imidacloprid at early signs of attack
Drench the base of plants with lambda-cyhalothrin to kill larvae in stems
Pull up and destroy collapsed plants to prevent adult emergence and further spread
prevention: Treat seed with thiamethoxam or imidacloprid seed dressing to protect young seedlings
Plant early in the season when fly populations are lower
Intercrop beans with non-host crops (maize, sorghum) to reduce pest density
imageDescription: Young plants wilt suddenly despite adequate soil moisture. Stems show swelling and discoloration at the base. Splitting the stem reveals white legless maggots tunnelling inside. Leaf mines appear as pale winding trails in the leaf tissue.

name: Aphids
symptoms: Colonies of small, soft-bodied insects clustered on growing tips and undersides of leaves
Leaves curl downward or inward around aphid colonies
Sticky honeydew deposits on leaves leading to black sooty mould growth
Distorted and stunted new growth
Yellowing of heavily infested leaves
treatment: Spray insecticidal soap solution (2% concentration) directly onto aphid colonies
Apply pyrethroid insecticides (cypermethrin) or neonicotinoids (imidacloprid) for severe infestations
Introduce or conserve natural enemies such as ladybird beetles and parasitic wasps
prevention: Inspect plants regularly, especially growing tips, for early aphid colonies
Avoid excessive nitrogen fertilisation which produces soft succulent growth attractive to aphids
Use reflective aluminium mulch to deter winged aphids from colonising plants
imageDescription: Dense colonies of tiny (1-3mm) green, black, or brown soft-bodied insects cluster on shoot tips and undersides of leaves. Leaves near colonies appear yellow, curled, and distorted. A sticky shiny residue (honeydew) covers affected leaf surfaces.

name: Whitefly
symptoms: Tiny white-winged adult flies rising in clouds when plants are disturbed
Pale yellow stippling on upper leaf surfaces from nymph feeding
Sticky honeydew secretion leading to sooty mould on leaves
Yellowing and premature drop of heavily infested leaves
Reduced plant vigour and stunted growth under heavy infestation
treatment: Apply imidacloprid, spiromesifen, or buprofezin insecticide at first detection
Use yellow sticky traps to monitor and reduce adult populations
Spray neem oil solution (2-3%) on undersides of leaves where nymphs feed
prevention: Install fine insect-proof netting (50-mesh) over seedbeds and young transplants
Intercrop with strongly scented plants (basil, marigold) to repel whiteflies
Remove and destroy crop residues promptly after harvest to eliminate breeding sites
imageDescription: Clouds of tiny (1mm) white moth-like insects fly up when plants are disturbed. Undersides of leaves are covered with flat, scale-like yellow to white nymphs. Upper leaf surfaces show yellow spotting and a shiny, sticky coating from honeydew.

name: Root Rot (Pythium/Fusarium)
symptoms: Reddish-brown discoloration and decay of the tap root and lower stem
Wilting and yellowing of leaves despite adequate soil moisture
Stunted plant growth with poor pod and seed set
Brown or black streaking visible when roots are examined
In severe cases, complete collapse and death of seedlings (damping-off)
treatment: Apply metalaxyl or mefenoxam drenches around the base of affected plants
Improve field drainage immediately by creating furrows or raised beds to reduce waterlogging
Remove and destroy severely affected plants to prevent soil-borne spore spread
prevention: Treat seed with metalaxyl + thiram seed dressing (e.g. Apron Star) before planting
Avoid planting in poorly drained or waterlogged soils; use raised beds where necessary
Practice a minimum 3-year crop rotation, avoiding all legumes on infected plots
imageDescription: Roots show reddish-brown to black rotting that extends up the lower stem. The cortex slips off the central root cylinder easily. Above-ground, plants wilt irreversibly even when watered. Seedlings may collapse at or just below the soil surface.
